meta.source: Chatbot Doula Edinburgh AI Generated Responses v5ST.docx

flow_description:
Contents

## HighLevel Flow

- Begin with Question 1 and proceed sequentially through Question 9

- Question 10 (SelfHarm) acts as a dynamic interrupt, assessed whenever triggered

- Responses are evaluated both individually and cumulatively

- Care recommendations escalate based on risk trajectory, not single answers alone

## Response Classification Logic

### Positive Responses

- Indicate typical postpartum adjustment

- No immediate concern

- Proceed to next question

### Moderate Responses

- Indicate clinically relevant symptoms

- Trigger followup questions

- Contribute to cumulative risk score

### Negative Responses

- Indicate severe distress, loss of function, or safety risk

- Trigger urgent or emergent escalation, depending on content

## QuestionByQuestion Logic (Q1–Q9)

### If response is POSITIVE

- Continue to the next unanswered question

- Maintain baseline monitoring

### If response is MODERATE

- Ask followup questions for that domain

- Classify followup responses as: 

- Positive

- Moderate

- Negative

- Update global risk profile:

#### ✅ Followup Positive

- Symptom appears mild, situational, or transient

- Continue screening

- No escalation required

#### ⚠️ Followup Moderate

- Symptom is persistent, draining, or increasing

- Mark domain as clinically active

- Continue screening and monitor accumulation

#### ❗ Followup Negative

- Symptom is pervasive or significantly impairing

- Flag for urgent clinical evaluation

- Screen immediately for safety (Question 10)

## Cumulative Risk Escalation (Q1–Q9)

### Escalate to URGENT clinical followup if:

- ≥3 domains show Moderate or Negative responses
OR

- Moderate responses include: 

- Persistent duration (most days, weeks or months)

- Functional impairment (difficulty caring for self or baby)

- Sleep disruption plus emotional symptoms
OR

- Symptoms show worsening trajectory

Urgent clinical followup includes:

- Sameweek (often sameday) mental health evaluation

- Support planning

- Continued safety monitoring

## Question 10: SelfHarm (Interrupt Question)

Question 10 may be:

- Asked directly at the end
OR

- Triggered immediately by concerning responses elsewhere

### If response is POSITIVE (No thoughts present)

- Confirm current safety

- Return to next question if any remain

### If response is MODERATE

Examples:

- Fleeting, passive, or intrusive thoughts

- No intent, but distress present

- Safety intact but requires effort

#### Required Actions:

- Ask followup questions to assess: 

- Frequency

- Intensity

- Controllability

- Intent

- Perceived safety

- CLASSIFY followup responses:

##### ✅ Followup Positive

(Fleeting, rare, controllable, safety intact)

- Continue screening

- Recommend early outpatient mental health support

- Plan repeat assessment

##### ⚠️ Followup Moderate

(Recurring, distressing, harder to control, no intent)

- Urgent escalation

- Recommend sameday or nextday professional evaluation

- Provide support and safety planning resources

- Do not defer or minimize

### If response is NEGATIVE

Includes:

- Frequent thoughts (daily or neardaily)

- Loss of safety

- Desire to be gone or not exist

- Feeling close to acting

- Inability to control thoughts

#### 🚨 Immediate Emergency Response

- Stop routine screening

- Initiate emergency protocol: 

- Crisis support

- Emergency services

- Immediate human handoff

- Do not continue chatbotled assessment

questions.title: Laugh/See the Funny Side
question_variants: Do you feel like you’ve been able to laugh as much as you’d like?
Have you been able to laugh and enjoy things as much as you usually do?
Have you been able to laugh as much as you normally would?
Have you been able to share laughter or notice funny moments recently?
Have you felt able to laugh and enjoy humor in the past week?
Have you felt able to laugh and see the funny side of things lately?
Have you found yourself laughing or noticing the funny side of things recently?
Have you noticed any changes in how often you laugh or find things funny?
Have you noticed humor or funny moments bringing you joy recently?
Have you noticed yourself laughing or finding things funny lately?

How much have you found yourself laughing or enjoying humor these days?

In the last few days, have you been able to enjoy humor the way you usually do?
Laughter can tell us a lot about how we’re doing. How has that been for you this week?
Many people find their sense of humor changes when they’re under stress. How has it been for you this week?
Over the past week, have you been able to laugh and see the funny side of things?
Sometimes laughter can be a good sign of how we’re feeling. How has that been for you this past week?
Thinking about your week, have you been able to laugh and see the funny side of things?

further_followup_for_green: “It sounds like things have felt really heavy for you. When you think about the past few days, has anything—even something small or fleeting—felt a little less heavy?”
“I hear how constant this feels. Are there moments, even brief ones, where things feel slightly more bearable?”
“It sounds exhausting to feel this way. Is there anything—people, routines, distractions—that gives you even a tiny bit of relief?”
“Given how overwhelming this has been, are there any parts of your day that feel even a little less difficult than others?”

“Hearing how constant and heavy this feels, I want to understand how you’re coping with all of this. Have you had any thoughts about not wanting to be here or wishing you could escape these feelings?”
“When things feel this overwhelming, some people start having thoughts about harming themselves or not wanting to keep going. Has anything like that been happening for you?”
“Given how intense this has been, I want to check in about your safety. Have you had any thoughts about hurting yourself or feeling like you don’t want to live?”
“With everything feeling so heavy, have you noticed any thoughts about wanting to disappear or not wake up?”

“It sounds like this has been weighing on you constantly. I want to understand the full picture—are there any moments that feel even slightly lighter, or has it gotten to the point where you’re having thoughts about not wanting to be here?”
“Hearing how unrelenting this feels, I want to check in on two things: whether anything still brings even a small sense of relief, and whether you’ve had any thoughts about harming yourself.”
“It sounds incredibly heavy. As you sit with all of this, do you notice any moments that feel a bit less overwhelming, or have you been having thoughts about wanting to escape or not be alive?”

examples: Any indication of self-harm or suicidality

examples: It doesn’t affect my day much
It sticks longer than I’d like
I always assume I’m the problem
I feel guilty most of the time

further_followup_for_green: “It sounds like these self‑blaming thoughts have been really constant. When they show up, do you ever notice even a small part of you that can push back or remind yourself it might not actually be your fault?”
“I hear how heavy this feels. Are there any moments—however brief—when you’re able to see things a bit more fairly toward yourself?”
“Given how strong these thoughts feel, are there times when you can still recognize that you’re doing your best, even if it doesn’t feel that way?”
“It sounds exhausting to feel responsible for so much. Are there any situations where you’re able to be a little gentler with yourself?”
“Hearing how constant and heavy this guilt feels, I want to understand how you’re coping. Have you had any thoughts about not wanting to be here or wishing you could escape these feelings?”
“When people feel this weighed down by self‑blame, they sometimes start having thoughts about harming themselves or not wanting to keep going. Has anything like that been happening for you?”
“Given how overwhelming this feels, I want to check in about your safety. Have you had any thoughts about hurting yourself or feeling like you don’t want to live?”
“With everything feeling so heavy and constant, have you noticed any thoughts about wanting to disappear or not wake up?”

“It sounds like this self‑blame has been weighing on you a lot. I want to understand the full picture—are there any moments where you can still see things more fairly toward yourself, or has it gotten to the point where you’re having thoughts about not wanting to be here?”

“Hearing how constant this guilt feels, I want to check in on two things: whether anything still helps you feel even a little more balanced, and whether you’ve had any thoughts about harming yourself.”

“It sounds incredibly heavy. As you sit with all of this, do you notice any moments that feel a bit more grounded or self‑compassionate, or have you been having thoughts about wanting to escape or not be alive?”

examples: Any indication of self-harm or suicidality
